speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Frieda W. Aaron, on February 5, 1995. The interviewer is Lisa Berlin. The city is Belle Harbor, New York, in the United States, and we'll be speaking in English. Frieda was born Fredzia, F-R-E-D-Z-I-A, Wakszlak, W-A-K-S-Z-L-A-K, on January 4, 1928, in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very complimentary of you, it's very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So are you going to tap me whenever you start rolling?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just start by saying your name and your age, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Frieda W. Aaron, and I'm 67 years old. I was just 67 last month.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Could you start by telling me about your life in Warsaw, while you were growing up, before the outbreak of World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I come from a middle-class family. My parents were not born in Warsaw. One was born in Russia, in Vitebsk, [BACKGROUND NOISE] that's my mother. And she fled Poland, I believe, during the revolution came to-- rather she fled Russia during the revolution, came to Pere. There, she met my father. And they both-- I guess they met in Warsaw. And although they came from Orthodox homes, they themselves were not Orthodox at all. And my father was a jeweler and also a chemist. There were only two in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the one who was allowed to determine the karat of gold. I don't know really how to explain it. What else shall I tell you about my family? I have a younger sister than I, who fortunately survived the war, as did my mother. We're, all three of us, all the time together. But I guess we'll get to it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a-- our parents were superb. I hope I'm not defying them, especially my father, who was killed in the most terrible way. But he really was a wonderful, wonderful father. He was-- as you may imagine, in Poland, during those days, and Europe in general, men did not play a significant role in raising their children. Well, actually, they did, in a different way from the way it is in the United States. But he was the only man who was, in my school, that is to say, who was like the head of the PTA. And that was most unusual. And he was very much involved in our education. He helped us with homework and he urged us to read. In fact, I begin reading at a rather early age. As a result of which I skipped first grade and I went into the second grade. Immediately, my sister learned from me. My sister was 18 months younger than I. Learned reading for me, but nobody believed that she was able to read. Everybody thought she was copying [LAUGHS] me. In another words, that she was looking at the pictures and just reading the pictures. But, actually, she was reading the words. We lived in-- in the Jewish quarters of Warsaw. We lived on Mila street, M-I-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your address?

speaker: interviewee
text: 58 Mila Street, which was a rather poor area. Because having come from various places of both Poland-- not various, but my father came from a small town in Poland, my mother came from Russia-- they had to really establish themselves. So they struggled for some time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a small town in-- in Poland. The name escapes me for some reason, probably come back to me. And as soon as I remember, [LAUGHS] I'll inject this information. Pinczow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, P-I-N-C-Z-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was actually in Galicia. It was near Krakow. So where was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: That they were struggling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were struggling initially, then things became better. But when we lived on Mila Street-- Mila actually, but we pronounce it in Polish, Mila. The L is like a W. And I went to a school that was quite distant, quite a distance from-- from the home, from my apartment house. And the school was a new school. And as I remember, it was a segregated school. I don't remember having seen any children other than Jewish children there. Although, many of the teachers were not Jewish, they were Christian. They were Pols, obviously. And the school was located in a very Christian area, in an Aryan area. And both my sister and I had to run from the beginning of the block, where the school was, to the very end because we're usually assaulted with stones. So this was 1938, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're very keenly aware of the anti-Semitism. It actually was a constant. There was a church across our street of which we were terrified. That was later, that was after we moved to Nowolipki. And, oh, if you want to know the spelling, N-O-W-I-L-I-P-K-I. Nowolipki, OK, No-wil-lipki, I suppose you would spell it. I hope I have the spelling correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we also-- when we lived there, we moved in 1938, I believe, 1939, at the beginning of 1939, we still attended the same school. On Spokojna Street. OK. I had very loving, very good parents. I had friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. She had an exquisite voice. And I imagine, if she had been born in the United States, she probably would have been a great opera singer. She had really an exquisite trained voice, her voice was trained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she sang an awful lot. She sang operas to us all the time, various arias. And she had a terrific sense of humor, very creative. She was always aware of beauty, of the sunset. She always called it to our attention of anything that was beautiful. She was very aesthetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in fact, when our children were growing up, she told each one of them-- that is, my sister's and my children-- she told each one of the children, you'll be a poet and you'll be a philosopher. She held this in very high regard, more so than being a physician or an attorney. [LAUGHS] What else shall I tell you about my life in Poland? Tell me about your apartment on Mila Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We lived on the fifth floor walk-up. Actually, you might call it fourth floor walk-up because the grounds level was never regarded. That's the first floor, it was parted. OK. It was a rather, well, poor. The tenants were rather poor. And we had-- you walked into the kitchen, from the kitchen, you walked into my father's room where he worked. And from there, into the living room, bedroom, they were all one, and dining room. We all slept in the same room. And nothing terrible happened to us, we didn't have a separate room. In fact, I think we're a rather healthy family, that is, psychologically sound family, despite the fact that we all slept in the same room. We had the radio, which was quite a thing. I think we didn't have a telephone on Mila. My mother was a very pedantic person with regard to cleanliness. Because, in Poland, things are not-- were not, I don't know how it is now, haven't returned to Poland, I imagine that the advancements are quite impressive. But it was very difficult to keep your body without lice, and bed bugs, and the beds because there was no running hot water. We had cold water and we didn't have a bathroom in our apartment. We had to go to a outhouse. But, of course, we children did not, we had potties, you know. So my mother used to take care of that. And then, we moved from there to a much, much, more comfortable apartment where we had a kitchen, a bathroom. Incidentally, as I mentioned, we didn't have the bathroom on Mila Street. On Nowolipki, we had a kitchen, a bathroom, a living room, dining room. And you walked into a hallway first, so that was quite luxurious and quite impressive. And then the living room, dining room, led into the bedroom, where we call slept, again. But it was a large bedroom, and there we had nice furniture. [LAUGHS] Much nicer than before. And then there was a third room that also was adjacent to the living room, this is where my father worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he conduct his-- his business--

speaker: interviewee
text: At home--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In both cases, in both apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people would come to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were-- people came. And I don't remember my parents having had any friends, but one, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but we had relatives. My mother had two sisters and were very, very close. I simply imagine that they may have had some friends. But life was very, very different from-- from life here, where people have friends. They have sometimes surrogate relatives because everybody tends to move away. But we're very close with our aunts, and uncles, and-- and cousins. We're like really-- really a family. It's perhaps difficult to translate it into contemporary terms because it's so different now. I imagine that it was different here years ago, too, 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see much of your grandparents when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually, both my maternal grandparents had died. No, what am I saying? My maternal grandmother died before I knew her. In fact, my sister was named after her. My grandfather, my mother's father, was a very Orthodox man, but a very-- I shouldn't say but, why but? And a very gentle, wonderful human being. I remember he had a long white beard and he wore a Jewish hat. I don't know whether you know what it looks like, you know, with a little visor. And we used to play with his beard. We used to take his hat off, under which he wore a yamaka. And he used to let us braid his-- his beard and twist it. And my mother used to take great pride in this. And in fact, he allowed my-- one of my younger cousins-- I had four cousins, four male cousins. Each of my aunts had two-- two sons, so there was six of us, in essence. My grandfather used to allow my youngest cousin to wear my grandfather's cap in the street, [LAUGHS] which was really quite a thing, you know. It was quite amusing. I want to go back to my father for a moment. In addition to being so concerned about our education and so loving, he also used to do some shopping, which was very unusual for men those days. When I'm talking about shopping, I mean groceries. You know, not too much, it was shameful. [LAUGHS] It was just, this was a woman's job, you know. But he used to-- and he-- he used to polish the floor. He used to-- well, we didn't have any mechanical polishers, polishing machines. He used to put on brushes on his feet that were tied. You know, like a rubber thing on top. Not a rubber, like a rubber band or thicker, you know, like-- and he used to slip into them and polish the floor this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHTER] And he had this terrific sense of humor, so did my mother. He was more serious than my mother. My mother had a wonderful sense of humor. She made everybody laugh always with her jokes. She used to tell us all sorts of stories, especially when she wanted us to eat. That was always an issue even though-- but we were never hungry, that's for sure. There was plenty of food. We didn't have oranges, bananas, you know, these exotic fruits. For us, they were exotic. Here, they're quite common. And she used to feed us. In fact, she would put us on a window so that we were distracted, in order to eat, because food was extremely important. Because, I guess, my mother, although she came from a very learned family, her father was a writer. He knew several languages, despite the fact that he, or because maybe of the fact that he had a beard and was extremely Orthodox. He was not a Hasidic, but a very, very Orthodox Jew. I lost the train of thought. Oh, yeah, my mother used to almost take chances with [LAUGHS] our lives in order that we would eat. Because I guess she must have been starving as a child. I'm assuming that, I'm not sure. But food for many Jewish people in Eastern Europe was extremely important. Eating a lot was extremely important, as much as one could. One was never concerned about weight. And I don't remember too many overweight people, for that matter. Not as a result of starvation, but simply maybe the nature of the food. Another ploy, another trick to make me eat, was to let me read. I used to love to read. So I would read, then she would shove the food into my mouth. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a maid, which was very common. Virtually everyone did because there were no washing machines or running water for that-- no, we did have cold running water in both apartments. And there were no washing machines or dishwashers, so one had to scrub, you know, the-- the washed in that huge-- I don't know what to call it. In Polish, it's [POLISH]. It's a big, round thing made of wood. Basin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like a basin. But, somehow, I envision a basin, it's not made of wood, but I suppose it is a form of a basin. I could look it up in the Polish dictionary. OK. And what else shall I tell you about? There is so much to tell, I don't know where to begin. I have these images scattered and I don't know how to pull them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents, yes. My grandmother, my father's mother, died also before I knew her. My grandfather lived in Pinczow. And I only met him once because I think that it was expensive to travel to Warsaw, and certainly for us to go to Pinczow. But I know that my parents corresponded with him. We had no telephone, so we could not contact each other by telephone. But I remember my grandfather came once to visit with us because he had some sort of problem, a problem with his-- one of his fingers. And I don't remember whether the finger was amputated or whether it was treated in the hospital in-- in Warsaw. I also had another cousin in another town, [NON-ENGLISH], it was the female cousin, and she came to visit us. She had graduated from gymnasium, which is high school, which is on a higher level, I suppose, than it is here, certainly currently. And she was about 18 years old. And I was extremely impressed with her elegance. And she met my male cousin, who was born in Warsaw, who was an absolutely brilliant young man. As a matter of fact, in 1939, he was the only Jew to have received a stipend to college. This was just before the war broke out, and they were rather poor people. I mean, not-- not abjectly poor, but relatively poor. They also had a tiny apartment, and-- and-- But he was-- both he and his-- his-- his brother were very bright. I want to interject something, I hope it's all right, because I'm afraid I'm going to forget. In fact, I wanted to show you a photograph. And I'm going, perhaps, to ask my husband to help me with it. These two brothers were-- one of them was a communist, the older one was a communist. And he was working very arduously and he was arrested. He was also an intellectual.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they related to you exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were my mother's sister's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So one of the-- they were two of the six that you mentioned before?

speaker: interviewee
text: All together, we're six children. Two girls, we were the youngest ones, my sister and I. And my-- I'll give them names, David, or Dudek, as we used to call him, and Yosek, which is Joseph. OK, so Joseph, or Yosek, was the communist. And he was married just before the war. And during the war-- that is not during the war. When the Germans entered Poland, when they begin to bomb Warsaw, they fled to Russia. Russia was the only country at the time that allowed, opened the doors, to Jews. And they prevailed upon my father to flee with them because my father was somewhat prominent, having been one of two people who were allowed to test the gold. So he was fairly well-known. And my father ultimately agreed, even though he didn't want to. He fled with them. This was during the bombing in September 1939. But after a week, he returned. You know, because he left the wife and two children, and so he just walked back. I guess I-- remind me to talk about it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Because there's something interesting. I just don't want to forget about these wonderful cousins of mine. So Dudek, the younger one, and Yosek and his wife, Techa, fled to Russia. And they went into the Soviet Union at the time, and they were in Bialystok. And, ironically enough, the Soviets allowed those people-- so the Soviets sent those people who wanted to return to Poland to Siberia as punishment. OK? But my cousins did not want to go back to Poland. They wanted to say there, even though their mother-- the beginning of the war, there was still some correspondence, letters were sent. And she pleaded with them to come back, and they decided not to. So when the pact between Germany and the Soviet Union was broken, they were all killed. So that's a tragic irony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin's wife, Techa, was pregnant at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. And they were very much part of your life growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Indeed. Yes, as a matter of fact, my younger cousin, Dudek, or David, taught me reading when I was about four years old. And this is why I skipped the first grade. And he also taught me to dance, he was a terrific dancer. And it so happened that I danced quite well, too. He used to show off with me and my sister to his friends. You know, a little girl, six, seven years old, and we're able to do the tango, and the waltz, and the foxtrot. And so he was very proud us and used to show off with us. And he teased us, of course, he was a teenager. [LAUGHS] Although, perhaps the teenagers were somewhat different there than they are here. I don't know. Yeah, I think so because one was more concerned with the nitty gritties of life than, you know, with the sorts of things that our children are concerned with, which is not to criticize them. That's, you know, a function of the culture. So the children in some way [LAUGHS] are totally guiltless. It's just, you know, culture, I suppose. So let's see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you always close with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, yes. I was jealous of her because she was a much better athlete than I was. She was a terrific athlete. And she is-- even now, she is a hiker and she skis. In fact, I like to hike, too. We went to the Canadian Rockies last summer. And on the first day, being 66 years old, I guess I can take some pride, we hiked 10 miles up a very steep mountain. And it was really glorious. Yeah. It was quite an exciting time. I hope this is not [LAUGHS] irrelevant to whatever we are saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Tell me about Judaism and the role that it played in your lives, and Jewish holidays, and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we're very observant Jews, only on a traditional level. That is to say, we-- we followed all the traditions, Passover, and-- and Hanukkah, and all the other holidays. But my parents did not go to the synagogue regularly. In fact, rather rarely. I don't really quite know why. I, you know, never-- being too young to ask and having-- never having had any very religious relatives, the notion of asking them why never even occurred to me. I just took it for granted. It's just now that I'm wondering. And I suppose they were, in a way, free thinkers. I don't know, they wanted to extricate themselves from the shtetl. I'm not sure. This is just guessing on my part, and I guess it's not to be taken seriously. [LAUGHTER] But my father used to take us to the synagogue on-- after Rosh Hashana-- Simchat Torah, because it was a very joyous holiday. And we had flags, and the children were marching with apples and, you know, candy. But we had, I guess, a more or less kosher home. I mean not to be kosher those days in Poland, certainly my class, you know, it was very unusual. Although, once, we went on a ride on the river, you know, in a boat, a boat ride on the river. And for the first time, I had a ham sandwich. So it was-- [LAUGHS] it was-- well, I don't know if it was a celebration, but it was certainly a very important event, a very unusual event. And I wasn't poisoned. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You lived to tell about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your school. It was-- it was a difficult journey there everyday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed. And in fact, one of the teachers was Polish. She was-- she taught music. And she was extremely anti-Semitic. I used to be blonde, but she called me redhead. And being called redhead-- I mean, red hair was not popular in Poland when I was a child. Allegedly, only Jewish people had red hair. So it was a terrible insult. But I also had some wonderful teachers. I remember one specifically who taught Jewish religion. And I used to get very much engrossed in it. I was very-- I like mythology, you know, and I-- I just loved it. And I had lots of friends in school. One of whom left Poland, just before the war, to Argentina. And I wanted always to contact her, but I just didn't know how because I don't remember her last name. So you know, I-- I often think of her. I don't know if I answered your question totally about the-- oh, yes, we studied Hebrew, my sister and I. But we did not study the Ashkenazi, that is the-- the Hebrew for-- for prayer, but rather the modern Hebrew, which is essentially Sephardic. So both my sister and I had four years of Hebrew. We had a tutor who came home. But I must confess, I really don't understand much, but I can read. And rather interestingly, when I was writing my book, never really learned to read Yiddish, I was able to use the Hebrew alphabet, because the Yiddish language has a Hebrew alphabet. And I was able to read the Yiddish poetry. And since I speak Yiddish and I understand it very well, and I speak it very well, I-- you know, that-- that having learned Hebrew was extremely useful in my translating the poetry. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they spoke Yiddish at home. And they spoke Polish to us. So we were bilingual immediately, even though we did not speak much Yiddish. I think the reason for it was the anti-Semitism. And if one spoke with a Yiddish accent, it was rather dangerous. So they wanted us to speak as pure a Polish as we possibly could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, these were stratagems people used [LAUGHS] to try to make life more tolerable, because the anti-Semitism, as I recall it, was universal. Certainly, where we lived. Although, we lived in the Jewish quarters, but we constantly heard about-- especially just before the war, the beards shorn off the faces of Orthodox people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I said, there was a church across the street from us. And even before the war, for a short period-- I don't remember precisely when we moved, in '38, or '39. I think probably the beginning of '39. During Easter, we were afraid to walk out of the house, you know, because they had-- I don't precisely what, I can't think of what to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Wait, one sec, while you-- let's have a pause then actually, while you're in between thoughts, because we need to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Am I too scattered? Should I be a little bit less scattered?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You're doing fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I just, you know--
